faults.field_trick: The CR30 LED indicator codes are printed on a label on the side of the unit. Red blinking patterns correspond to specific fault channels. Count the blinks to identify which input caused the fault â€” saves time versus connecting CCW software.

faults.name: Allen-Bradley POINT I/O Module Fault (1734 Series)
faults.description: POINT I/O module (1734 series) has faulted. Module status LED shows red or module is not communicating with the I/O adapter. Associated I/O points are invalid.
probable_causes: Module not fully seated on DIN rail adapter base â€” partial insertion causes intermittent backplane contact
Module internal failure â€” electronics failed, status LED shows solid red
POINT I/O adapter (1734-AENT, 1734-AENTR) lost network connection to PLC
Module base terminal block wiring fault â€” shorted or open field wiring pulling module into fault
Module firmware version incompatible with I/O adapter firmware
Power supply to POINT I/O system is low or failed â€” modules require 24VDC system power
fix_steps: Identify faulted module â€” check status LEDs on all modules in the POINT I/O bank, red=fault
Reseat the faulted module â€” lift latch, remove, re-insert onto base firmly until it clicks
Check 24VDC power to the POINT I/O bank â€” measure at power supply terminals
Check 1734-AENT adapter status â€” network LED should be solid green, module status should be green
Disconnect field wiring from the faulted module terminal block â€” if fault clears, the issue is in the field wiring
Compare module catalog number and firmware to the PLC I/O configuration â€” must match
If module is dead (no LEDs at all), check the base terminal block for 24VDC â€” replace module if power is present but no response
faults.field_trick: POINT I/O modules have a removable terminal block base. You can pull the module off the base without disconnecting field wiring. Snap a new module onto the existing base â€” all wiring stays intact. This makes swaps very fast in the field.

faults.name: Allen-Bradley ControlLogix I/O Module Fault (1756 Series)
faults.description: ControlLogix 1756 series I/O module has faulted. Module may show red status LED or is reported faulted in the PLC I/O tree. Associated I/O channels are invalid.
probable_causes: Module not fully seated in the ControlLogix chassis backplane â€” partial insertion causes loss of backplane communication
Module internal hardware failure â€” ASIC, relay, or analog circuitry failed
Field wiring issue â€” shorted sensor wiring pulling an input channel into fault
Module firmware needs update â€” incompatible with current controller firmware level
Chassis backplane connector damaged â€” bent pin or contamination on connector
Module configuration does not match physical module â€” wrong module type in I/O tree
fix_steps: In RSLogix 5000, check I/O tree for the faulted module â€” right-click > Properties > General shows fault status
Reseat the module â€” open retention latches, pull module straight out, re-insert firmly until latches click
Check module status LED â€” green=OK, red=fault, flashing red=needs update or config error
If input module fault, try disconnecting field wiring â€” if fault clears, issue is in field wiring (shorted sensor, etc.)
Verify module catalog number matches what is configured in the I/O tree â€” 1756-IB16 is not 1756-IB32
Update module firmware using ControlFlash if firmware mismatch is indicated
If module is dead after reseat, swap to a different chassis slot to rule out backplane connector issue

faults.name: Allen-Bradley EtherNet/IP Adapter Fault (1756-EN2T / 1756-EN2TR)
faults.description: ControlLogix EtherNet/IP communication module has faulted or lost connections. Remote I/O racks, VFDs, and network devices connected through this module are offline.
probable_causes: Module not fully seated in the ControlLogix chassis backplane
Ethernet port link lost â€” cable disconnected, switch port down, or cable damaged
Module IP address conflict â€” another device on the network has the same IP
Module connection table full â€” too many CIP connections configured through this module
Module firmware crash â€” needs power cycle to recover
DLR (Device Level Ring) break detected on 1756-EN2TR â€” ring topology has an open segment
fix_steps: Check module front panel LEDs â€” Network Status (NS) should be green, Module Status (MS) should be green
Check Ethernet cable connections at both ports â€” verify link LEDs on module and switch
Reseat the module in the chassis if MS LED is off or red
Verify IP address using module rotary switches or RSLinx â€” check for conflicts on the network
Power cycle the module by removing and re-inserting it â€” allows firmware to restart
If using DLR ring topology on EN2TR, check ring status â€” one cable break is OK (ring becomes linear), two breaks cause isolation
Check connection count in module diagnostics â€” reduce connections or distribute across multiple EN2T modules if at limit

faults.name: Allen-Bradley PanelView HMI Communication Fault
faults.description: PanelView or PanelView Plus HMI lost communication with the ControlLogix/CompactLogix PLC. Screen displays communication error or tag values show as '####' or 'Comm Err'.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable between HMI and PLC (or network switch) disconnected or damaged
PLC went to fault mode or was taken offline â€” HMI cannot read tags
HMI application shortcut tag path incorrect after PLC controller name change
Network switch port failure between HMI and PLC
HMI IP address changed or conflicts with another device
PLC EtherNet/IP module overloaded â€” too many HMI connections competing for resources
fix_steps: Check Ethernet cable at HMI and switch â€” verify link LED on both ends
Verify PLC is in RUN mode â€” check keyswitch or RSLogix 5000
Ping PLC IP address from HMI terminal settings or from a laptop on the same network
In FactoryTalk View ME/SE, check RSLinx Enterprise shortcut paths â€” controller name must match PLC
Restart the HMI application â€” press Ctrl+Alt+Del on PanelView Plus to access Windows desktop if needed
If multiple HMIs on the network, check for IP conflicts â€” each must have a unique IP
Power cycle the HMI as last resort â€” hold power button or cycle the 24VDC supply

faults.name: Allen-Bradley Flex I/O Module Fault (1794 Series)
faults.description: Flex I/O module (1794 series) has faulted. Module may show red status LED. Typically found in remote I/O racks connected via DeviceNet, EtherNet/IP, or ControlNet.
probable_causes: Module not fully seated on the Flex I/O backplane terminal base
Module internal hardware failure
Field wiring fault â€” shorted or open wiring on an I/O channel
1794-AENT or 1794-ADN adapter lost communication with PLC
24VDC power supply to the Flex I/O bank is low or failed
Module configuration mismatch between physical module and PLC I/O tree
fix_steps: Check module status LED â€” green=OK, red=fault, off=no power or dead
Reseat the module on its terminal base â€” push firmly until it locks in place
Check 24VDC power to the Flex I/O bank power supply â€” measure at terminals
Check adapter module (1794-AENT for EtherNet/IP or 1794-ADN for DeviceNet) status LEDs
If field wiring suspected, disconnect wiring from the faulted module â€” if fault clears, trace the field wiring
Verify module catalog number matches the PLC I/O configuration
If module is dead, swap with a spare â€” the terminal base retains all field wiring for fast swap

faults.name: Allen-Bradley Micro800 PLC Fault (Micro820/850)
faults.description: Micro820 or Micro850 controller has faulted or stopped running. Status LED shows red or program execution halted. These compact PLCs are used for standalone machine control.
probable_causes: Program download incomplete or corrupted â€” program needs re-download from Connected Components Workbench
I/O expansion module fault â€” plug-in module not responding or misconfigured
24VDC power supply voltage out of range â€” below 19.2V or above 28.8V
Memory full â€” program too large for controller memory or data table overflow
Embedded I/O channel fault â€” wiring short on built-in I/O point
Controller firmware corrupted â€” needs firmware reload via USB
fix_steps: Check controller status LED â€” solid green=running, flashing green=program stopped, red=fault
Connect with Connected Components Workbench (CCW) via USB or Ethernet â€” read the fault status
Verify 24VDC power supply â€” measure at controller terminals, must be 19.2V to 28.8V
If program fault, re-download the program from CCW â€” verify project matches the controller
Check all plug-in expansion modules â€” reseat any modules with red LEDs
If firmware corrupted, use CCW to reload firmware via USB connection
Check field wiring on embedded I/O â€” disconnect suspect wiring and test

faults.name: PowerFlex VFD Fault â€” Parameter Error / Factory Reset Needed
faults.description: PowerFlex drive reports parameter error, EEPROM fault, or parameters are at factory defaults after unexpected reset. Drive configuration is lost or corrupted.
probable_causes: Drive EEPROM write failure â€” parameter storage corrupted by power loss during parameter save
Someone performed a factory reset (Parameter A099 = 1) accidentally or intentionally
Firmware update cleared drive parameters â€” backup not restored after flashing
Drive replacement â€” new drive from stock has factory default parameters, not application parameters
Communication adapter pushed factory defaults to drive â€” configuration mismatch
fix_steps: Check if a parameter backup exists â€” look for a .pf525 or .pf527 parameter file saved during commissioning
If backup available, download parameters using Connected Components Workbench (CCW) or DriveExplorer
If no backup, retrieve parameter sheet from the original commissioning documents or integrator
At minimum, reprogram: Motor NP Volts (A089), Motor NP Hz (A090), Motor NP Amps (A091), Accel Time (A091), Decel Time (A092)
Set communication parameters to match the PLC configuration â€” IP address, EtherNet/IP assembly instances
After reprogramming, do Parameter A099 = 2 (Save to EEPROM) to lock in the new parameters
Test motor operation at low speed before running at full speed â€” verify rotation direction and speed reference source
faults.field_trick: Always keep a backup of every drive's parameters. Save a .pf525 file after commissioning and store it in the panel door pocket or on the plant's engineering PC. A drive swap goes from a 2-hour emergency to a 15-minute swap when parameters are backed up.

faults.name: 440C-CR30 Input Channel Fault
faults.description: The CR30 detected a fault on one or more safety input channels. Dual-channel input wiring fault, cross-fault, or discrepancy between Channel A and Channel B input signals. All safety outputs de-energized.
probable_causes: Cross-fault detected â€” Channel A and Channel B wires shorted together (common in cable bundles)
Single-channel input wired to a dual-channel (Category 3/4) function â€” incorrect wiring for SIL level required
Input device failed in one channel only â€” NC contact welded or open on one side of a dual-channel E-stop
Discrepancy timeout exceeded â€” both channels of E-stop did not open/close within the configured time window
Input cable damaged â€” insulation failure causing channel-to-channel short
Wrong input terminal used â€” wired to a terminal not mapped in the CCW configuration
fix_steps: Note the CR30 LED blink pattern on the front of the unit â€” count blinks to identify which input channel faulted
With the CCW software connected, go to Diagnostics tab to see exact fault description and channel number
Disconnect field wiring at the CR30 terminal for the faulted channel â€” if fault clears, fault is in field wiring or device
Measure continuity between Channel A and Channel B wires at the CR30 terminal â€” any continuity indicates a cross-fault
Test the safety input device (E-stop, gate switch) manually â€” verify both NC contacts open and close simultaneously
If discrepancy timeout fault: check that both channels of the safety device are actuated within the configured time window
After wiring is corrected, re-download CR30 configuration from CCW and reset the unit
faults.field_trick: Use a multimeter on the field wiring before reconnecting â€” measure resistance between the two channel wires with everything disconnected. Should read open (infinite). Any resistance below 100kÎ© is a cross-fault waiting to happen.

faults.name: 440C-CR30 Output Relay Fault
faults.description: The CR30 detected a fault on a safety output relay or semiconductor output. Output feedback check failed at startup or during operation. Safety outputs cannot be energized.
probable_causes: Output relay contact welded â€” CR30 detected output still active when it should have de-energized (feedback NC contact stuck open)
External contactor auxiliary (feedback) contact wiring fault â€” feedback loop not correctly wired back to CR30 feedback input
Overloaded output â€” output relay contact carrying more current than rated (max 6A AC1 per output relay)
Short circuit on output wiring â€” output terminal shorted to ground or to another terminal
Output relay failed open â€” output de-energized when it should be active (relay coil open)
Wrong output type used â€” relay output wired where semiconductor output is required (or vice versa)
fix_steps: Check CR30 LED â€” solid red output LED indicates output relay or semiconductor output fault
Measure the external contactor or actuator connected to the output â€” verify it is de-energized and its auxiliary contact is closed
Measure feedback wiring back to CR30: the feedback input should read 24VDC when contactor is de-energized
Check output load current â€” verify load does not exceed CR30 output rating (6A per relay output, 0.5A per semiconductor output)
Disconnect the output load â€” if fault clears, the load is causing the fault (overload, short, or weld)
If relay welded, the CR30 module requires replacement â€” internal safety relays are not field serviceable
If feedback wiring issue, correct wiring and re-download CR30 configuration from CCW
faults.field_trick: Before assuming the CR30 is bad, measure at the contactor: the normally-closed aux contact on the contactor should be CLOSED (continuity) when the contactor coil is de-energized. If it reads open, the contactor itself is welded â€” not the CR30.

faults.name: 440C-CR30 Configuration Error
faults.description: CR30 safety relay configuration is missing, corrupted, or does not match what is expected. Unit boots in a faulted state with no safety outputs active. Requires CCW software to re-download the safety configuration.
probable_causes: Configuration lost after battery backup failure â€” CR30 has no internal battery; configuration stored in non-volatile memory that was corrupted
CR30 replaced with a new unit â€” new units from stock have no configuration loaded
Configuration CRC mismatch â€” existing configuration file on PC does not match what was last downloaded to the CR30
Firmware update cleared configuration â€” after a firmware flash, configuration must be re-downloaded
CCW software locked configuration to specific serial number â€” replacement unit requires configuration unlock and re-download
fix_steps: Connect to CR30 via USB cable using Connected Components Workbench (CCW) software
In CCW, go to the Safety Configuration view â€” check if configuration is present in the CR30 or blank
Retrieve the CCW project file (.ccwproj) from the plant's engineering PC or backup drive
Download (Write) the configuration to the CR30 in CCW â€” verify CRC matches after download
If no CCW file exists, the configuration must be rebuilt from the original safety design or commissioning documents
After successful download, lock the configuration in CCW using the Safety Lock function
Perform a full functional test of all safety inputs and outputs per the plant's safety validation procedure
faults.field_trick: Keep the CCW project file (.ccwproj) saved in a folder on the panel door USB drive AND on the plant server. A CR30 replacement with no CCW file on site means rebuilding from scratch â€” this can take a safety engineer a full day.

faults.name: 440C-CR30 Power Supply Fault
faults.description: CR30 supply voltage is out of specification. The CR30 requires 24VDC nominal (range 19.2â€“28.8V). Below or above this range causes the unit to de-energize outputs and fault.
probable_causes: 24VDC power supply voltage sagged below 19.2V â€” power supply overloaded or failing
24VDC supply voltage exceeded 28.8V â€” power supply regulation failure or incorrect supply used
Excessive voltage drop on the 24VDC wiring from supply to CR30 â€” long cable run, undersized wire, or loose terminal
24VDC fuse blown on the safety circuit feed
Multiple devices on same power supply overloaded the supply, causing voltage sag
fix_steps: Measure 24VDC directly at the CR30 power input terminals (not at the power supply output) â€” must be 19.2â€“28.8V
Compare measurement at CR30 vs. at power supply output â€” large difference indicates wiring/connection issue
Check all terminals on the 24VDC safety circuit for tightness â€” loose terminals cause voltage drops under load
Check 24VDC fuse or breaker protecting the CR30 supply circuit
If supply is overloaded, identify all devices on the same supply and verify total current draw is within supply rating
Replace the 24VDC power supply if its output is out of specification under load
faults.field_trick: Measure voltage AT the CR30 terminals, not at the supply. A 1V drop over a cable run at 5A is normal Ohm's law â€” you might see 24.5V at the supply and 22V at the CR30. This is a wiring/terminal issue, not a supply issue.

faults.name: 440C-CR30 EtherNet/IP Communication Fault
faults.description: The CR30 EtherNet/IP safety connection to the GuardLogix or CompactGuardLogix controller has been lost or timed out. CIP Safety communication interrupted. Safety outputs may be de-energized depending on the configured connection timeout behavior.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable disconnected or damaged between CR30 and managed switch or GuardLogix
Managed switch port failed or VLAN configuration blocking CIP Safety traffic
IP address conflict â€” another device on the network has the same IP as the CR30
GuardLogix controller faulted or rebooted â€” CIP Safety connections dropped
Network traffic overload causing missed packets â€” CIP Safety has strict timing requirements
CR30 IP address or connection parameters changed in CCW without updating GuardLogix configuration
fix_steps: Check the CR30 NET LED â€” solid green is normal, flashing green is connected/active, red is fault
Ping the CR30 IP address from the engineering PC â€” confirm network connectivity
Check the Ethernet cable and RJ45 connector at the CR30 â€” inspect for damage and reseat
In RSLogix 5000, check the I/O tree for the CR30 module â€” look for connection faulted status
Verify the CR30 IP address matches what is configured in the GuardLogix I/O module definition
Check for IP address conflict using the Rockwell BOOTP/DHCP server tool
If switch port, swap to a known-good port and retest
After resolving network issue, reset the safety fault in GuardLogix
faults.field_trick: If the CR30 NET LED is green but GuardLogix still shows a connection fault, check for an IP conflict first. Two devices with the same IP on the same subnet causes sporadic comm faults that are hard to trace.

faults.name: 440C-CR30 Dual-Channel Discrepancy Error
faults.description: A dual-channel safety input (E-stop, gate switch, safety mat) did not have both channels activate or de-activate within the configured discrepancy time window. CR30 interprets this as a potential channel failure and de-energizes outputs.
probable_causes: Safety input device has one slow or sticky contact â€” NC contact on one channel is sluggish
Input wiring to one channel has higher resistance than the other â€” different voltage levels reaching CR30 inputs
Discrepancy time window too tight â€” configured time window is shorter than the device's mechanical actuation time
One channel of the E-stop button is mechanically worn and does not actuate consistently
User releasing and re-pressing E-stop quickly â€” partial actuation triggers discrepancy
Cable connector not fully seated on one channel â€” intermittent connection
fix_steps: In CCW Diagnostics, identify which input channel pair has the discrepancy fault
Manually actuate the safety device slowly while watching both channel LED indicators on the CR30
If one LED lags the other, the slow channel has a worn contact or wiring resistance issue
Test the input device contacts with a multimeter â€” both NC contacts must open simultaneously when actuated
Temporarily increase the discrepancy time window in CCW to confirm if timing is the issue
Replace the input device if one contact is worn or sluggish â€” E-stop mushrooms are consumable parts
Check connector seating on both input channels and reseat firmly
faults.field_trick: Press the E-stop slowly and watch the CR30 input LEDs. Both should go off at the same time. If one flickers off then back on before the other, that button needs replacement. Slow actuation reveals contact quality issues that fast actuation hides.

faults.name: 440C-CR30 Manual Reset Required
faults.description: The CR30 is configured for manual reset. After a safety input was activated (E-stop, guard door, light curtain), all inputs have returned to the safe/reset-ready state but the system requires a deliberate manual reset before safety outputs will re-energize.
probable_causes: E-stop was pressed and released â€” machine is now in reset-required state (normal and expected behavior)
Guard door was opened and re-closed â€” machine requires reset per safety design
Light curtain was interrupted and is now clear â€” reset required before motion can resume
Power was cycled â€” CR30 always requires manual reset after power-up when configured for manual reset
Reset button wiring fault â€” CR30 is not seeing the reset signal when button is pressed
fix_steps: Walk the machine and verify all E-stops are released (twist-and-pull to release), all guard doors are closed, all light curtains are clear
Locate the machine's safety reset button (typically green pushbutton on the control panel or at a remote station)
Press and hold the reset button for 1â€“2 seconds â€” CR30 requires a minimum pulse width to accept the reset signal
Verify the CR30 OUTPUT LED changes from amber/off to green after reset â€” indicates outputs energized
If reset button press does not clear the condition, check wiring from reset button back to CR30 reset input terminal
Verify reset button NC/NO contact type matches CR30 configuration in CCW
If CR30 still does not reset, check that all safety inputs are truly in their de-actuated state â€” CR30 will not reset with any input in fault
faults.field_trick: Operators often push the reset button too quickly. The CR30 requires a 0.25â€“2 second pulse to register a valid reset. Hold the button for a full 2 seconds to ensure the CR30 accepts it. A momentary tap is often ignored by the relay.

faults.name: VFD Overcurrent Trip
faults.description: Drive tripped on instantaneous overcurrent. Output current exceeded the hardware limit, shutting down to protect IGBTs.
probable_causes: Short circuit in motor cable — check with megger between phases and to ground
Motor windings shorted — measure phase-to-phase resistance with motor disconnected
Incorrect motor parameters — verify nameplate amps match drive settings
Mechanical load jam — motor cannot turn, stall current exceeds OC threshold
IGBT module degrading — drive trips at low loads where it previously ran fine
Cable too long without output reactor — capacitive charging current triggers OC
fix_steps: Disconnect motor cables at drive output terminals — if fault clears on start, problem is external
Megger motor cables: phase-to-phase and phase-to-ground, must be >1 MΩ
Check motor windings with LCR meter — phase balance within 5%
Verify drive parameter: motor rated current matches nameplate FLA
Inspect for mechanical binding — try to turn motor shaft by hand
Check acceleration time — too fast ramp causes current spike
If cable run >100ft, add output line reactor (3-5% impedance)
Replace drive if IGBT test fails (many drives have self-test in diagnostics menu)
faults.field_trick: Disconnect motor leads at the drive. If it still faults on start with nothing connected, the drive IGBTs are shot.

faults.name: VFD DC Bus Overvoltage
faults.description: DC bus voltage exceeded safe threshold. Typically caused by regenerative energy from the motor during deceleration.
probable_causes: Deceleration time too short — motor regenerates energy faster than bus can absorb
Braking resistor disconnected or open — no path to dissipate regen energy
Incoming line voltage too high — measure at drive input terminals
Large inertia load — motor acts as generator during coast-down
Braking transistor failed — cannot switch regen energy to resistor
fix_steps: Increase deceleration time — double it and test
Verify braking resistor: measure resistance, check wiring to drive BR terminals
Measure incoming voltage at drive input — should be within ±10% of drive rating
Enable coast-to-stop if application allows — eliminates regen entirely
Check drive DC bus voltage on parameter display — should be ~1.35x line voltage at rest
For high-inertia loads, size braking resistor for continuous duty
Verify braking transistor with drive diagnostic test
faults.field_trick: Set decel time to 999 seconds temporarily. If OV goes away, it's a regen problem. Size a braking resistor.

faults.name: VFD DC Bus Undervoltage
faults.description: DC bus voltage dropped below minimum operating threshold. Drive cannot maintain output to motor.
probable_causes: Incoming power sag or brownout — facility-wide voltage dip
Loose connection on input power terminals — arcing causes intermittent drops
Input fuses partially blown — one phase open
Contactor feeding drive has bad contact — check all 3 phases with meter
DC bus capacitors degrading — cannot hold voltage during transients
fix_steps: Measure voltage at drive input terminals under load — all 3 phases within 3%
Tighten all input power connections — check torque specs on terminal blocks
Check input fuses with continuity test, not just visual inspection
Verify contactor contacts — measure voltage drop across each pole under load
If DC bus caps are swollen or leaking, drive needs repair/replacement
Add ride-through feature if available in drive parameters
Consider adding a line reactor on input to improve power quality
faults.field_trick: Put your meter on the drive input terminals and watch for voltage sag when nearby big motors start. If it dips >15%, that's your UV source.

faults.name: VFD Heatsink Overtemperature
faults.description: Drive heatsink temperature exceeded safe limit. Internal thermal protection activated to prevent component damage.
probable_causes: Cooling fan failed — check if fan spins freely and runs when drive is powered
Heatsink fins clogged with dust/debris — restrict airflow
Ambient temperature too high — drive enclosure in direct sun or near heat source
Drive overloaded continuously — running above rated current for extended time
Ventilation inadequate — insufficient clearance above and below drive
fix_steps: Check cooling fan operation — spin by hand, verify power to fan
Clean heatsink fins with compressed air — blow from inside out
Verify ambient temp is within drive spec (typically 40°C/104°F max)
Check motor current vs drive rating — derate if ambient >40°C
Ensure minimum clearances: typically 6" top/bottom, 2" sides
Replace fan if bearings are noisy or rotation is sluggish
If in enclosed panel, add cabinet cooling fan or A/C unit
faults.field_trick: Hold your hand 6 inches from the heatsink exhaust. If you can't feel airflow, the fan is dead or blocked. #1 cause of VFD overtemp.

faults.name: VFD Ground Fault
faults.description: Drive detected current flow to ground on output side. Motor cable or motor insulation has broken down.
probable_causes: Motor winding insulation failure — phase shorted to frame
Cable insulation damaged — pinched, cut, or worn through in conduit
Water/moisture intrusion in motor junction box or cable tray
Improper cable routing — output cables too close to sharp edges
VFD output filter or reactor has internal ground fault
fix_steps: Disconnect motor cables at drive — megger test phase to ground (>1 MΩ good)
If cable passes, disconnect at motor and test motor alone — megger at motor terminals
Inspect motor junction box for moisture, corroded connections, or wire damage
Check cable routing in conduit — look for pinch points, sharp edges, tight bends
If output reactor installed, disconnect and test separately
Dry out motor if moisture suspected — use space heater or low-voltage energize
faults.field_trick: Megger test at the drive first. If it fails, go to the motor end and test cable alone vs motor alone. The bad one will be obvious.

faults.name: VFD Input Phase Loss
faults.description: Drive detected loss of one or more input power phases. Single-phasing causes DC bus ripple and potential damage.
probable_causes: Input fuse blown on one phase — check all three
Loose wire on one phase of the input terminal block
Upstream breaker has a weak pole — trips one phase intermittently
Supply transformer has an open winding on one phase
Contactor feeding drive has one bad contact
fix_steps: Measure all 3 input voltages at drive terminals — all should be within 3%
Check and tighten all input terminal connections
Test all input fuses with a meter — not visual, blown fuses can look intact
Inspect contactor contacts — look for pitting, measure contact resistance
If breaker suspected, load-test each pole or replace breaker
Check upstream connections at MCC or panel board
faults.field_trick: Clamp each phase at the drive input with a current meter. The missing phase will read zero or near-zero amps.

faults.name: VFD Motor Overload
faults.description: Electronic motor overload protection tripped. Motor drawing more than rated current for extended duration.
probable_causes: Mechanical binding in driven equipment — motor working harder than normal
Motor bearings failing — increased friction load
Motor overload parameter set too low — doesn't match motor FLA
Loose or broken belt/coupling — motor cycling on unloaded/overloaded
Ambient temperature causing motor to run hotter
Product jam or material buildup on conveyor/equipment
fix_steps: Check drive display for actual motor amps — compare to motor nameplate FLA
Verify motor overload parameter matches motor nameplate FLA
Check mechanical load — try to turn motor shaft by hand with equipment
Inspect motor bearings — listen for grinding, check bearing temperature
Clear any product jams or material buildup
If motor is old, check winding resistance — degraded windings draw more current
Consider upsizing motor if load has increased permanently
faults.field_trick: Compare drive amps to motor nameplate. If amps are at or above FLA at what used to be a light load, suspect bearings or mechanical binding.

faults.name: VFD Bus Fault
faults.description: Internal DC bus fault detected. Bus charging circuit, capacitors, or pre-charge resistor may have failed.
probable_causes: Pre-charge resistor open — bus cannot charge through soft-start circuit
Bus capacitors failed — swollen tops, leaking electrolyte
Bus charging relay/contactor stuck open
Internal fuse blown on DC bus
Power supply board failure
fix_steps: Power down completely, wait 5 min for caps to discharge
Measure DC bus voltage at test points — should be ~1.35x line voltage
Inspect capacitors visually — look for bulging tops or leakage
Check pre-charge resistor with ohmmeter — compare to spec
Listen for charging relay click on power-up
If bus won't charge, drive likely needs factory repair or replacement
faults.field_trick: After full power-down and discharge, measure DC bus at the test points. If it reads zero or very low when powered up, the pre-charge circuit is dead.

faults.name: VFD Communication Loss
faults.description: Drive lost communication with PLC or network controller. Commands cannot be sent or received over the fieldbus.
probable_causes: Network cable disconnected or damaged — check both ends
Communication module not seated properly in drive slot
Network node address conflict — two devices with same address
PLC scanner module fault — check scanner status
Baud rate mismatch between drive and network
Termination resistor missing on last device in network
fix_steps: Check network cable connections at drive and PLC/switch — reseat both ends
Verify communication module is fully seated and locked in drive
Check network LED indicators — link/activity lights should be solid/blinking
Verify drive node address is unique — check against network device list
Confirm baud rate/speed setting matches network configuration
For Modbus/serial: check wiring polarity (A/B), termination on last device
For EtherNet/IP: verify IP address, subnet, gateway settings
faults.field_trick: Swap to local control mode on the drive keypad. If motor runs fine in local, the problem is 100% communication — not the drive or motor.

faults.name: VFD Parameter Error
faults.description: Drive detected an invalid parameter configuration. One or more settings conflict or are out of valid range.
probable_causes: Firmware update changed parameter ranges — old values now invalid
Manual parameter entry error — wrong value typed in
Parameter file loaded from different drive model
EEPROM corruption after power surge or static discharge
Motor nameplate values entered incorrectly
fix_steps: Read the specific parameter flagged in the fault detail
Check parameter against valid range in drive manual
Run motor auto-tune if available — recalibrates motor parameters
If widespread corruption, restore factory defaults and re-enter parameters
Save known-good parameters to file/keypad memory as backup
faults.field_trick: Factory default the drive, then run auto-tune with the motor connected. This fixes 90% of parameter errors.

faults.name: VFD Stall Detection
faults.description: Drive detected motor stall condition — current at max but speed not increasing. Motor is locked or severely overloaded.
probable_causes: Mechanical jam — product, debris, or foreign object blocking driven equipment
Motor bearings seized — shaft cannot rotate
Coupling failure — motor turning but load isn't moving
Stall detection parameter too sensitive for the application
Belt or chain snapped — motor reaches speed but no load feedback
fix_steps: Check mechanical load — attempt to rotate motor/equipment shaft by hand
Clear any jams, debris, or foreign objects
Check motor bearings — spin shaft by hand, feel for roughness or grinding
Verify coupling between motor and load is intact
Adjust stall detection threshold if nuisance trips on normal load surges
Check belt/chain tension and condition
faults.field_trick: Disconnect the coupling and start the motor unloaded. If it runs fine, the problem is downstream mechanical, not the motor or drive.

faults.name: VFD Encoder Feedback Error
faults.description: Drive lost feedback from motor encoder. Closed-loop speed control cannot maintain accuracy without feedback.
probable_causes: Encoder cable damaged, pinched, or disconnected
Encoder failed — no pulses being generated
Encoder power supply voltage incorrect
Electrical noise on encoder signal — cable not shielded or routed near power cables
Encoder resolution setting doesn't match actual encoder PPR
fix_steps: Check encoder cable connections at motor and drive — reseat connectors
Verify encoder power: typically 5V or 24V at encoder connector
Slowly rotate motor by hand — watch for pulse count on drive display
Inspect encoder cable for damage — must be shielded, routed away from power cables
Verify encoder PPR setting in drive matches physical encoder
If noise suspected, check shield grounding — ground at drive end only
faults.field_trick: Disconnect encoder and switch drive to open-loop (V/Hz) mode. If motor runs fine, the encoder or its wiring is the problem.

faults.name: PLC Major Recoverable Fault
faults.description: ControlLogix/CompactLogix processor encountered a major fault that can be cleared without reprogramming.
probable_causes: Watchdog timer expired — program execution exceeded time limit
Stack overflow — excessive subroutine nesting or recursion
Invalid memory access — array index out of bounds
Math overflow — result too large for data type
Motion group fault — servo drive reported error to processor
fix_steps: Check fault code in Controller Properties > Major Faults tab in Studio 5000
Fault Type 4 Code 16 = watchdog: increase task watchdog time or optimize program
Fault Type 4 Code 20 = stack overflow: check for recursive JSR calls
Fault Type 4 Code 34 = motion fault: check axis faults in motion group
Clear fault and switch from Faulted to Run mode
If fault recurs immediately, the root cause is still present
faults.field_trick: Go online with Studio 5000, right-click the controller icon, select Properties, then Major Faults tab. It tells you EXACTLY which instruction and routine caused the fault.

faults.name: PLC I/O Module Fault
faults.description: An I/O module in the local or remote chassis has faulted. Module may have lost communication or reported an internal error.
probable_causes: Module not seated properly in chassis backplane
Module power supply failed — LED indicators dark or flashing red
Backplane connection corrupted — dirty or damaged pins
Module firmware mismatch with controller
Field wiring short causing module to fault
RPI (Requested Packet Interval) too fast for the network
fix_steps: Check module LED status: green=OK, red=fault, off=no power
Reseat module — remove and firmly reinsert into chassis slot
Check module properties in I/O tree for specific fault code
Verify module firmware matches project configuration in Studio 5000
Inspect backplane connector for bent pins or contamination
Check field wiring for shorts — disconnect field wires and see if fault clears
If remote I/O, check EtherNet/IP cable and switch connections
faults.field_trick: Swap the faulting module with an identical one from a non-critical slot. If the fault follows the module, it's bad. If it stays in the slot, it's a backplane issue.

faults.name: EtherNet/IP Connection Timeout
faults.description: PLC lost EtherNet/IP connection to a remote device. Communication timed out waiting for response.
probable_causes: Network cable unplugged or damaged
Ethernet switch port failed or switch lost power
Remote device powered off or in fault
IP address conflict on the network
RPI set too aggressive for network traffic load
Multicast storm — too many devices on flat network
fix_steps: Check physical cable at both ends — look for link LED on switch port
Ping remote device IP from a laptop on the same subnet
Check remote device for power and local faults
Verify no IP address conflicts — scan network with Wireshark or RSLinx
Increase RPI from 10ms to 20ms or 50ms to reduce network load
If using unmanaged switch, verify port speed autonegotiation
Check for IGMP snooping on managed switches to reduce multicast traffic
faults.field_trick: Bring a laptop with RSLinx. If you can browse to the device but the PLC can't, it's an RPI/connection timeout setting, not a cable problem.

faults.name: PLC Battery Low
faults.description: Processor battery is low or depleted. Program and data retention at risk during power loss.
probable_causes: Battery has reached end of life — typical life is 2-3 years
High ambient temperature accelerated battery drain
Processor has been in storage without power for extended period
Battery connector not fully seated
fix_steps: Replace battery while processor is powered ON to prevent data loss
Use correct battery: 1756-BA1 for ControlLogix, 1769-BA for CompactLogix
After replacement, clear battery low bit in controller properties
Record replacement date on label attached to processor
Verify program auto-save is configured as backup
faults.field_trick: ALWAYS replace the battery with the PLC powered up. If you pull the old battery with power off, you may lose the program.

faults.name: PowerFlex Fault Code F2 - Auxiliary Input
faults.description: PowerFlex drive Fault 2: External fault input is active. An auxiliary contact or safety device is signaling a fault condition.
probable_causes: E-stop circuit open — button pressed or wiring fault
External overload relay tripped (if wired to auxiliary input)
Safety relay output contact open
Door interlock switch open on drive enclosure
Wiring error — aux input not jumpered when not used
fix_steps: Check what is wired to the auxiliary fault input terminal on the PowerFlex
If E-stop, verify all E-stop buttons are released and circuit is complete
If external overload, investigate motor overload condition and reset
Verify safety relay output is closed
If aux input is not used, jumper it per PowerFlex installation manual
Reset fault via keypad or digital input after clearing cause
faults.field_trick: Look at terminal 27 (or check the drive manual for your model). Whatever's wired there is what's faulting. If nothing is wired, it needs a jumper.

faults.name: PowerFlex Fault Code F33 - Auto Rstrt Tries
faults.description: PowerFlex drive exhausted all automatic restart attempts. Drive faulted repeatedly and gave up trying to restart.
probable_causes: Underlying fault keeps recurring — drive restarts then faults again
Intermittent motor short — works briefly then trips on ground fault or overcurrent
Power quality issue — voltage sags cause repeated UV faults
Mechanical load cycling between free and jammed states
Auto restart parameters too aggressive
fix_steps: Check fault log/queue on drive — the fault BEFORE F33 is the real problem
Fix the underlying recurring fault (usually OC, OV, UV, or GF)
Check motor and cables for intermittent problems
Review auto restart settings: number of tries, delay between tries
If power quality issue, add line reactor or investigate facility power
Manual reset required after F33 — clearing the root cause is mandatory
faults.field_trick: F33 is never the real fault. Scroll back in the fault log — the fault right before it is what you need to fix.

faults.name: PLC Redundancy Switchover
faults.description: ControlLogix redundancy pair performed a switchover. Secondary processor took over from primary.
probable_causes: Primary processor encountered unrecoverable fault
Redundancy chassis link cable disconnected
Primary power supply failed
Manual switchover initiated by operator
Primary processor firmware crash
fix_steps: Check which processor is now primary in Controller Properties
Inspect original primary for fault codes — resolve root cause
Verify redundancy link cable is secure between chassis
Check power supply status on both chassis
If switchover was unexpected, investigate and fix before clearing alarm
faults.field_trick: The blue LINK LED on both 1756-RM2 modules should be solid. If one is off, the redundancy link is broken.

faults.name: PowerFlex 525 F7 - Motor Overtemp
faults.description: PowerFlex 525 detected motor over-temperature via thermal model or thermistor input.
probable_causes: Motor running above rated current for extended period
Ambient temperature too high for motor service factor
Motor cooling fan blocked or failed (TEFC motor)
Thermistor parameter enabled but thermistor not connected
Motor thermal model parameters don't match actual motor
fix_steps: Check actual motor temperature with IR thermometer
Verify motor current on drive display vs nameplate FLA
Clean motor cooling fins and verify fan rotation (TEFC motors)
If using thermistor, verify wiring to drive analog input
If not using thermistor, disable thermistor fault parameter
Check motor thermal model settings match motor nameplate data
faults.field_trick: Hit the motor with an IR temp gun. If it's actually cool, the thermal model parameters are wrong — not a real overtemp.

faults.name: CompactLogix Nonvolatile Memory Fail
faults.description: CompactLogix processor detected a failure in nonvolatile (flash) memory. Program storage may be compromised.
probable_causes: Flash memory chip reaching end of write-cycle life
Power interruption during program save/download
Static discharge damaged memory chip
Firmware update interrupted
Manufacturing defect
fix_steps: Download a known-good backup of the program to the processor
If download fails, attempt firmware flash recovery
Contact Rockwell tech support for RMA if memory is physically failed
Ensure UPS protects PLC during firmware updates and program saves
Replace processor if memory recovery is not possible
faults.field_trick: If you can get online but can't download, try a firmware downgrade then re-upgrade. Sometimes this resets the flash controller.

faults.name: Point I/O Adapter Communication Loss
faults.description: 1734 Point I/O adapter lost communication with the ControlLogix/CompactLogix processor over EtherNet/IP.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable disconnected at adapter or switch
Switch port failure
Adapter IP address changed or conflicts
Power supply to Point I/O rack failed
RPI too aggressive for network load
fix_steps: Check Ethernet cable link LED at adapter — must be solid green
Verify adapter power — module status LED should be green
Ping adapter IP from laptop on same network segment
Check for IP conflicts using RSLinx network browse
If on DLR ring, check ring status for open ring condition
Increase RPI if network is heavily loaded
faults.field_trick: The LINK led on the 1734-AENT adapter tells you everything. Green solid = cable good. Off = cable or switch issue. Flashing = traffic but no controller connection.

faults.name: Conveyor Belt Tracking Error
faults.description: Belt has migrated off-center and is rubbing against the frame or triggering a tracking sensor. Can cause belt damage if not corrected.
probable_causes: Belt splice not square — misaligned splice causes belt to walk
Conveyor frame not level — belt drifts to low side
Idler rollers not square to frame — crowned rollers worn flat
Material buildup on rollers — causes diameter difference that steers belt
Loading point off-center — material weight pulls belt to one side
Take-up tension uneven — one side tighter than other
fix_steps: Observe which direction belt tracks — adjust steering idler opposite to drift direction
Clean all rollers, especially carry-side idlers — remove built-up material
Check frame level with torpedo level — shim if needed
Inspect belt splice — should be perpendicular to belt travel direction
Adjust return idlers first (tail end), then carry-side idlers, working toward head
Verify loading point is centered on belt — adjust chute or skirtboard
Re-tension take-up evenly — equal snug on both sides
If belt is permanently stretched on one side, belt needs replacement
faults.field_trick: Adjust ONE idler at a time, 1/4 turn. Wait for the belt to make 3 full revolutions before adjusting again. Overadjusting is the #1 tracking mistake.

faults.name: Conveyor E-Stop Active
faults.description: Emergency stop circuit is open. Conveyor drive is disabled. One or more E-stop devices are activated or circuit has a fault.
probable_causes: E-stop button pressed — check all stations along conveyor line
Pull-cord switch activated — cable pulled or switch triggered
Safety relay failed or not reset after E-stop event
Wiring fault in E-stop circuit — open connection in series chain
Safety PLC input module fault
fix_steps: Walk the entire conveyor line — check every E-stop station for pressed buttons
Check pull-cord switches — reset if activated, inspect cable tension
Reset safety relay after all E-stops are released
If no visible activation, check E-stop circuit continuity with meter
Verify safety relay output contacts are closing after reset
Check PLC safety input module for faulted channel
faults.field_trick: In a long conveyor line with many E-stops, use a meter at the safety relay input. Start at the relay and work backward, checking circuit continuity at each junction box until you find the open.

faults.name: Conveyor Motor Overtemperature
faults.description: Conveyor drive motor temperature has exceeded alarm threshold. Motor protection tripped to prevent winding damage.
probable_causes: Overloaded conveyor — too much product weight on belt
Motor cooling fan blocked or failed
Ambient temperature in plant is excessive
Motor bearings failing — added friction generates heat
VFD running motor at low speed for extended time — reduced cooling
fix_steps: Reduce belt loading — spread product out or reduce throughput temporarily
Check motor fan — clean debris, verify fan spins freely
Measure motor surface temp with IR gun — compare to nameplate temp rise
Check motor bearings — listen with stethoscope or vibration pen
If running at low speed continuously, switch to inverter-duty motor with forced cooling
Allow motor to cool down before restarting — minimum 30 minutes
faults.field_trick: If the motor surface is too hot to hold your hand on for 3 seconds, it's over 60°C and probably above Class B insulation rating. Don't restart until it cools.

faults.name: Conveyor Photoeye Blocked
faults.description: Photoeye sensor continuously in blocked state. Product may be stuck, sensor may be misaligned, or wiring fault.
probable_causes: Product jammed at sensor location — physical obstruction
Photoeye lens dirty — dust, grease, or moisture
Photoeye misaligned — emitter and receiver not pointing at each other
Sensor cable damaged or disconnected
Retro-reflector missing, fallen off, or dirty (retro-reflective type)
Sensor sensitivity set too high — picking up background objects
fix_steps: Clear any product jam at the sensor location
Clean sensor lens with soft cloth — both emitter and receiver if through-beam
Realign sensor — use alignment indicator LED on sensor body
Check wiring connections at sensor and PLC input module
Clean or replace retro-reflector (if retro-reflective type)
Adjust sensitivity trim pot — start low and increase until reliable
Replace sensor if LED indicators show fault after cleaning and realignment
faults.field_trick: Cover the emitter with your hand. If the PLC input doesn't change, the problem is wiring/PLC, not the photoeye. If it does change, realign or clean.

faults.name: Conveyor Chain Stretch Alarm
faults.description: Chain-driven conveyor reports excessive chain elongation. Take-up travel is at maximum or chain has stretched beyond adjustment range.
probable_causes: Normal chain wear — chains stretch over time and need replacement
Lubrication inadequate — dry chain wears faster
Overloaded conveyor — excessive weight accelerates wear
Chain and sprocket mismatch — wrong pitch or worn sprockets
Corrosive environment eating chain material
fix_steps: Measure chain stretch — compare 10-link section to new chain spec
If >3% elongation, chain needs replacement
Inspect sprocket teeth for hooking (worn into hook shape) — replace with chain
Adjust take-up to re-tension — if at max travel, chain is too long
Check lubrication system — verify oil/grease is reaching chain pins
When replacing chain, always replace sprockets at the same time
faults.field_trick: Lift the chain off the sprocket. If you can pull it away from the sprocket tooth more than half a tooth pitch, it's stretched and needs replacement.

faults.name: Conveyor Accumulation Zone Full
faults.description: Accumulation conveyor zone has reached capacity. Upstream zones should stop to prevent product collisions.
probable_causes: Downstream process stopped or slowed — products backing up
Zone release sequence malfunction — PLC logic not advancing products
Zone sensor stuck in blocked state (see CONV-004)
Discharge end product not being removed — manual station unmanned
Timing/spacing between zones not tuned for product flow rate
fix_steps: Check downstream equipment — restart if stopped
Verify zone release PLC logic is executing properly
Check zone sensors — clean and verify each zone sensor operates correctly
Clear products at discharge end if manual operation
Review zone timing parameters — adjust gap time between zone releases
If multiple zones full, work from discharge end backward
faults.field_trick: Start diagnosing at the DISCHARGE end, not the infeed. In accumulation conveyors, the problem is almost always downstream — something isn't taking product away.

faults.name: Conveyor Speed Deviation
faults.description: Actual belt speed differs from commanded speed by more than allowable tolerance. Speed feedback doesn't match setpoint.
probable_causes: Speed sensor (encoder/prox) failed or misaligned
Belt slipping on drive pulley — tension too low
Drive pulley lagging worn smooth — no grip on belt
VFD not reaching commanded frequency — current limit active
Gearbox slipping or damaged — input and output speeds don't match
fix_steps: Check speed sensor: verify pulses with oscilloscope or drive display
Inspect belt tension — increase take-up if belt is slipping on drive pulley
Check drive pulley lagging — replace if smooth, worn, or delaminating
Check VFD display — if current-limited, reduce load or upsize drive
Listen to gearbox for unusual noise — check oil level and condition
Verify speed feedback scaling in PLC matches physical sensor setup
faults.field_trick: Mark the belt and drive pulley with a paint pen. If the belt mark moves relative to the pulley mark, the belt is slipping — re-tension or replace lagging.

faults.name: Sortation Diverter Fault
faults.description: Sortation diverter (pop-up wheel, pusher, or shoe sorter) failed to actuate or did not reach position within time limit.
probable_causes: Pneumatic cylinder not actuating — air supply issue
Solenoid valve stuck or failed — check coil and spool
Position confirm sensor not seeing diverter in position
Mechanical obstruction preventing full diverter travel
PLC output not energizing — check output module LED
fix_steps: Check air supply — gauge should read plant spec (typically 80-90 PSI)
Manually actuate solenoid valve — use manual override button on valve
Check position confirm sensors — verify they see diverter at both retracted and extended
Inspect diverter mechanism for jammed products or debris
Check PLC output — verify output LED is ON when divert commanded
If valve good but cylinder slow, check flow controls and cylinder seals
faults.field_trick: Hit the manual override on the solenoid valve. If the diverter moves, the problem is electrical (PLC output or solenoid coil). If it doesn't move, it's pneumatic or mechanical.

faults.name: Conveyor Metal Detector Trip
faults.description: Inline metal detector triggered on product passing through. Product has been rejected or line has stopped for inspection.
probable_causes: Actual metal contamination in product — requires investigation
Metal detector sensitivity set too high — false positives
Product effect — moisture, salt, or mineral content in product triggers detector
Vibration from conveyor causing false readings
Metal detector calibration drifted
fix_steps: Retrieve rejected product — inspect for visible metal contamination
Run test samples (ferrous, non-ferrous, stainless steel) through detector
If false positive, recalibrate metal detector per manufacturer procedure
Adjust sensitivity — start at minimum and increase to meet spec
Check conveyor for vibration — tighten mounting bolts, check motor balance
Verify reject mechanism is actuating properly and confirmed by sensor
faults.field_trick: Send the same product through 3 times. If it trips every time, it's real contamination. If it's intermittent, it's likely product effect or vibration.

faults.name: Conveyor Belt Splice Failure
faults.description: Belt splice has separated or is showing signs of failure. Belt may have visible gaps, fraying, or delamination at splice point.
probable_causes: Splice has reached end of life — normal wear
Excessive belt tension stretching splice apart
Belt overloaded — product weight exceeding belt rating
Improper splice technique or materials used in last repair
Belt tracking issue causing uneven wear on splice
fix_steps: Stop conveyor immediately if splice is separating — belt can break
Inspect splice — if >25% separated, do not run; repair or replace
Schedule belt splice repair with proper vulcanizing kit or mechanical fasteners
Check belt tension — over-tensioning accelerates splice failure
Verify belt rating matches actual load requirements
After repair, track belt to ensure it runs straight
faults.field_trick: Mechanical fasteners (Flexco clips) get you running in 30 minutes. Vulcanized splices last 3x longer but need 4+ hours. Pick based on urgency.

faults.name: AS/RS Crane Position Error
faults.description: Automated storage/retrieval crane reports position mismatch. Actual position does not match commanded location within tolerance.
probable_causes: Position encoder fault — missed pulses or lost reference
Crane hit obstruction during travel — products overhanging from rack
Homing sequence interrupted by fault or E-stop
Motor drive fault caused uncontrolled stop
Position calibration data corrupted in PLC
fix_steps: Re-home the crane from the designated home position
Inspect rack face for protruding products or damaged uprights
Check travel encoders — verify pulse count on manual jog
Verify home position sensors are clean and functional
Review position calibration table in PLC/WMS — compare to physical measurements
If encoder replaced, recalibrate all bay positions
faults.field_trick: Jog the crane to a known physical reference point (like bay 1, level 1) and compare the encoder reading to what the PLC expects. The difference tells you how far off calibration is.

faults.name: AS/RS Load Overweight
faults.description: Load weight exceeds maximum rated capacity for the crane or rack location. Load was rejected or crane stopped before storing.
probable_causes: Pallet loaded beyond weight spec by warehouse personnel
Weight sensor calibration drift — reading higher than actual
Tare weight offset incorrect — empty carrier not zeroed
Moisture absorption in product increasing weight over time
Wrong SKU assigned to location — lighter item expected
fix_steps: Remove pallet from crane and weigh on floor scale to verify
If actually overweight, repalletize to meet weight spec
If weight is within spec, recalibrate crane load cells
Zero tare weight with empty carrier on crane
Verify WMS has correct weight data for the SKU
faults.field_trick: Put a known weight (like a full water drum — ~450 lbs) on the crane and check the reading. If it's off by more than 2%, recalibrate the load cells.

faults.name: AS/RS Shuttle Transfer Fault
faults.description: Shuttle or transfer car failed to complete pallet handoff. Load is between crane and rack or between shuttle positions.
probable_causes: Shuttle rail misaligned with crane — docking sensors not confirming alignment
Pallet or load shifted during transfer — overhanging edge caught on guide
Transfer mechanism (chain, belt, or roller) not running — motor/drive fault
Load presence sensors not detecting pallet in correct position
Shuttle communication timeout with crane controller
fix_steps: Verify load position — visual inspection from safe location
Check docking sensors at transfer point — clean and realign if needed
Manually jog transfer mechanism to complete or reverse the transfer
Inspect pallet for damage or shifting that would cause a snag
Check transfer motor and drive for faults
Verify communication link between crane and shuttle controllers
faults.field_trick: Most ASRS transfer faults are caused by shifted loads. Look for product overhanging the pallet edge before troubleshooting anything electrical.

faults.name: AS/RS Fire Suppression Activated
faults.description: Fire suppression system in AS/RS rack area has activated or pre-alarm triggered. All crane operations suspended.
probable_causes: Actual fire — smoke or heat detected in rack area
Smoke detector false alarm — dust, steam, or HVAC blowing near detector
Sprinkler head damaged by crane or product — leaking
Linear heat detection cable damaged
Fire panel in trouble mode — wiring fault or dirty detector
fix_steps: EVACUATE AREA and call fire department if actual fire suspected
If false alarm confirmed, reset fire alarm panel per site procedure
Check smoke detectors — clean with compressed air if dusty
Inspect sprinkler heads along crane travel path for physical damage
Review fire panel event log for specific zone/device that triggered
Do NOT restart crane operations until fire marshal or safety manager approves
faults.field_trick: If it's a VESDA system (aspirating smoke detection), check the filter. A clogged filter causes false alarms. Replace filter and fault often clears.

faults.name: AS/RS Aisle Occupancy Fault
faults.description: Safety system detected personnel or unauthorized presence in the crane aisle. Crane locked out until aisle is confirmed clear.
probable_causes: Personnel entered aisle through safety gate without proper lockout
Safety light curtain blocked — debris, spider web, or misalignment
Safety gate interlock switch stuck in open state
Maintenance mode not properly activated before entry
Safety scanner detecting reflection from new/moved object in aisle
fix_steps: Verify aisle is clear — visual confirmation required by authorized person
Check safety gate interlocks — close gate, verify switch contacts close
Inspect light curtains — clean lenses, check alignment, clear obstructions
If safety scanner, check for new reflective surfaces in aisle that weren't there before
Reset safety system per site lockout/tagout procedure
Do NOT bypass safety system — ever
faults.field_trick: Safety light curtains catch spider webs constantly. A quick wipe with a dry cloth on both emitter and receiver sides clears 80% of aisle occupancy faults.

faults.name: Packaging Line Product Jam
faults.description: Product jam detected at packaging station. Products accumulated, overlapped, or wedged causing line stoppage.
probable_causes: Product orientation incorrect at infeed — rotated or flipped
Conveyor speed mismatch between adjacent sections — products collide
Guide rails too narrow or too wide — products tip or wedge
Photoeye timing incorrect — products released at wrong interval
Wear parts (pushers, diverters, star wheels) worn beyond tolerance
fix_steps: Clear the jam carefully — watch for product under spring pressure
Check product orientation at infeed — adjust guides or orienters
Verify conveyor speeds match between sections — use tachometer
Adjust guide rail width — 1/16" to 1/8" wider than product on each side
Check and adjust photoeye timing — watch product flow in manual/jog mode
Inspect and replace worn guide rails, pushers, or star wheels
Run 10 products through manually to verify proper flow before restarting
faults.field_trick: Slow the line down to half speed. If jams stop, it's a timing/speed issue. If jams continue at half speed, it's a mechanical alignment problem.

faults.name: Case Erector Vacuum Fault
faults.description: Case erector reports vacuum loss at suction cups. Cannot pick and open cardboard blanks for case forming.
probable_causes: Vacuum pump not running or underperforming
Suction cups worn, cracked, or wrong type for carton material
Vacuum hose kinked, disconnected, or leaking
Cardboard blank too dusty — vacuum cannot seal against surface
Vacuum level switch calibrated too high — nuisance fault
fix_steps: Check vacuum pump — verify it's running and gauge reads spec (typically -20" Hg)
Inspect suction cups — replace if worn, cracked, or deformed
Check all vacuum hoses for kinks, cuts, and secure connections
Clean suction cups and cardboard blank contact area
Verify vacuum level switch threshold setting — adjust per machine spec
If using venturi generators, check air supply pressure (80-90 PSI typical)
faults.field_trick: Wet your finger and press it over a suction cup while the pump is running. If you feel strong suction, the cups are fine and the blank surface is the problem. Try a different carton lot.

faults.name: Stretch Wrapper Film Break
faults.description: Stretch wrap film broke during wrapping cycle. Pallet may be partially wrapped or unwrapped.
probable_causes: Film tension too high — pre-stretch ratio too aggressive
Film quality issue — thin spots, wrinkles, or edge tears in roll
Sharp product edge cutting film during rotation
Film carriage idler rollers not spinning freely — grabbing film
Pre-stretch rollers worn or glazed — film sticking and tearing
Pallet load has protruding objects catching and tearing film
fix_steps: Rethread film through the carriage per machine manual
Reduce pre-stretch setting by 25% and test
Inspect film roll for damage — switch to a new roll if defective
Check all idler rollers in film carriage — spin by hand, should rotate freely
Clean pre-stretch rollers with solvent to remove film residue
Inspect pallet load for sharp edges or protruding items
Verify film gauge matches machine specification
faults.field_trick: If film breaks at the same wrap height every time, something on the load at that height is cutting it. Look for sharp corners, staples, or strapping.

faults.name: Packaging Line Servo Fault
faults.description: Servo motor or servo drive on packaging machine has faulted. Motion control lost on one or more axes.
probable_causes: Servo drive reported overcurrent — mechanical jam on axis
Encoder feedback lost — encoder cable or encoder failed
Following error exceeded — actual position too far from commanded position
Servo drive overtemperature — check cooling fan and ambient temp
Power supply to servo drive interrupted
fix_steps: Check servo drive display for specific fault code
Clear fault and attempt reset — if jam was temporary, drive may restart
Check for mechanical binding on the faulted axis — rotate by hand if safe
Verify encoder cable connections at motor and drive
Check drive cooling fan and heatsink temperature
If following error, check mechanical coupling between motor and load
Review servo drive fault log for pattern — intermittent faults suggest loose connections
faults.field_trick: Most servo faults on packaging machines are mechanical — jammed product, worn bearings, or broken coupling. Clear the mechanism first before diagnosing the drive.

faults.name: Label Applicator No Label
faults.description: Label applicator reports label out or no label detected at application point. Products passing through unlabeled.
probable_causes: Label roll empty — end of roll reached
Label peeled off backing but stuck to peel plate — not transferring
Label sensor not detecting label edge — dirty or misadjusted
Label roll loaded incorrectly — labels upside down or routing wrong
Pneumatic tamp cylinder not extending — label stays on pad
fix_steps: Check label supply — replace roll if empty
Verify label routing through machine matches diagram in service manual
Clean label sensor lens — use compressed air or soft cloth
Adjust label sensor position — edge of label must cross sensor beam
Check pneumatic tamp — verify air supply and solenoid operation
If labels curling off peel plate, slow down peel speed or adjust peel plate angle
Run a few labels in manual/jog mode to verify proper feeding
faults.field_trick: If labels are peeling but not applying, the peel plate angle is probably wrong. Sharper angle = labels peel off more aggressively. Adjust 2-3 degrees at a time.

faults.name: Palletizer Pattern Error
faults.description: Palletizer unable to build correct pattern layer. Products not in expected position or orientation for current pattern.
probable_causes: Product infeed timing incorrect — products arriving too fast or slow
Pattern recipe incorrect in PLC/HMI — wrong product selected
Row forming guides misaligned — products not squaring up
Pusher or sweep arm not completing full stroke
Product dimensions don't match recipe — different SKU on line
fix_steps: Verify correct pattern recipe is selected on HMI for current product
Check product dimensions against recipe specification
Adjust infeed conveyor speed/timing to match palletizer cycle
Inspect row forming guides — adjust to product dimensions + 1/8" gap
Verify pusher/sweep arm full stroke — check pneumatic cylinders and limit switches
Run pattern in manual/step mode to identify where products misposition
faults.field_trick: Run ONE layer in step mode, watching each pusher and guide movement. The step where products go wrong is where you focus your adjustment.

faults.name: Palletizer Pallet Dispenser Empty
faults.description: Pallet magazine/dispenser is empty. No pallets available for next load build cycle.
probable_causes: Pallet magazine actually empty — needs refill by forklift operator
Pallet sensor not detecting remaining pallets — misadjusted
Pallet dispenser mechanism jammed — pallet stuck between dispensing forks
Pallet size incorrect — wrong pallets loaded in magazine
Dispenser forklift interlock preventing refill
fix_steps: Check pallet magazine — refill if empty
Inspect pallet sensor — clean and verify it detects pallet presence
Clear any jammed pallets in dispenser mechanism
Verify pallet dimensions match dispenser specification
Check forklift interlock gate — close if left open
If sensor faulty, use override to continue while ordering replacement
faults.field_trick: Stack pallets NEATLY in the magazine. Crooked pallets are the #1 cause of dispenser jams. If the top pallet is crooked, they'll all jam.

faults.name: Palletizer Height Overtravel
faults.description: Elevator/lift table exceeded maximum height limit. Safety switch or encoder detected position beyond normal range.
probable_causes: Layer count exceeded — more layers than recipe allows
Height sensor/encoder missed counts — thinks it's lower than actual
Layer sheet or slip sheet jammed adding extra height
Product is taller than spec — different SKU loaded
Lift table overrun — drive decel too slow to stop at limit
fix_steps: Verify layer count matches recipe on HMI
Check product height against recipe specification
Inspect for jammed slip sheets adding extra height
Verify height encoder is reading correctly — compare to physical measurement
Check high limit switch — must stop lift reliably
Adjust lift deceleration if overrunning the stop point
faults.field_trick: Count the layers on the pallet. If there's one extra layer, the layer counter in the PLC is off by one — probably a missed or double-counted photoeye pulse.

faults.name: Safety Light Curtain Fault
faults.description: Safety light curtain reports internal fault or alignment error. Machine is locked out until fault is cleared.
probable_causes: Emitter and receiver misaligned — vibration or physical impact
Lens dirty — dust, grease, or moisture on optical surface
Cable damage on emitter or receiver unit
Internal fault — component failure in light curtain unit
External light source interference — direct sunlight or other light curtain
fix_steps: Clean both emitter and receiver lenses with soft dry cloth
Check alignment indicators on both units — must show aligned
Realign units if needed — follow manufacturer alignment procedure
Inspect cables for damage — especially at connector and flex points
Shield from external light sources if interference suspected
If internal fault, replace the faulted unit (emitter or receiver)
faults.field_trick: Most light curtain faults are dirty lenses or misalignment from vibration. Clean first, realign second, replace last.

faults.name: Safety Relay Channel Fault
faults.description: Dual-channel safety relay detected a discrepancy between channels. Cross-fault or timing mismatch between redundant inputs.
probable_causes: One E-stop contact block stuck — one channel open, one closed
Wiring error — channels reversed or crossed
Contact timing mismatch — one contact opens before the other beyond tolerance
Safety relay internal fault — output monitoring detected discrepancy
Safety device (gate switch, pressure mat) has one bad channel
fix_steps: Check safety device wiring — Channel 1 and Channel 2 must match diagram
Test E-stop contacts individually — both NC contacts must open and close together
If gate switch, verify both reed switches or contact blocks operate
Reset safety relay after clearing fault — observe if fault returns
If timing mismatch, replace the safety device — contact blocks degrading
Check safety relay diagnostic LED pattern for specific fault
faults.field_trick: Put your meter on the safety relay monitoring terminals. Check voltage on each channel — if one reads 24V and the other reads 0V, you know exactly which channel has the fault.

faults.name: Guard Door Interlock Open
faults.description: Machine guard door interlock switch indicates door is open. Machine is locked out from automatic operation.
probable_causes: Guard door actually open — close and latch securely
Interlock switch actuator misaligned with switch body
Interlock switch failed — contacts not closing when actuated
Door hinge sagging — actuator no longer reaching switch
Wiring fault between switch and safety relay/PLC
fix_steps: Close and latch the guard door securely
Verify interlock switch actuation — watch LED indicator on switch body
Realign switch actuator if needed — adjust mounting bracket
Check door hinge — tighten or replace if sagging
Test switch with meter — continuity when actuator is engaged
Check wiring from switch to safety relay input
Replace switch if mechanical actuation is correct but no electrical output
faults.field_trick: If the switch LED comes ON when the door closes but the PLC doesn't see it, the problem is wiring between the switch and the PLC, not the switch itself.

faults.name: Power Distribution Fault
faults.description: Main or branch power distribution fault detected. One or more circuits have tripped or voltage is out of specification.
probable_causes: Main breaker tripped on overload — too many loads starting simultaneously
Branch breaker tripped — single circuit fault
Utility power dip or outage — facility-wide event
Transformer overloaded — exceeding kVA rating during peak
Loose buss connection generating heat and eventually tripping
fix_steps: Check main and branch breakers in MCC/power panel — identify which tripped
Measure voltage at main buss — all phases should be within 2%
If overload trip, identify which loads were starting and stagger startup
Check transformer temperature — should not exceed nameplate temp rise
Inspect all buss connections with IR camera — look for hot spots
If utility issue, contact power company and check facility transfer switch
faults.field_trick: After a power event, walk the floor and count what's down. If EVERYTHING tripped, it's utility-side. If only one section, it's your internal distribution.

faults.name: Compressed Air System Low Pressure
faults.description: Plant compressed air pressure below minimum operating threshold. Pneumatic equipment may malfunction or stop.
probable_causes: Air compressor tripped or in fault — check compressor panel
Large air leak in distribution piping
Air dryer not cycling — moisture causing downstream issues
Demand exceeds compressor capacity — too many pneumatic loads
Air receiver tank drain stuck open — bleeding pressure
Regulator failure at main header
fix_steps: Check compressor status — restart if safe to do so
Walk the air line and listen for leaks — mark and repair
Check main pressure gauge at compressor discharge — should be 100-125 PSI
Verify air dryer is cycling — check dew point indicator
Check automatic drain valves — stuck open drains bleed pressure fast
If demand exceeds capacity, stagger pneumatic equipment startup
Check main header regulator — set to plant spec (typically 80-90 PSI downstream)
faults.field_trick: Shut down all pneumatic equipment on a weekend and watch the compressor. If it cycles on frequently with nothing running, you have a major leak to find.

faults.name: HMI Communication Loss
faults.description: HMI/operator panel lost communication with PLC. Screen shows communication error or stale data.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable disconnected between HMI and switch
HMI IP address changed or conflicts with another device
PLC in fault mode — not responding to HMI requests
Network switch port failure
HMI application crashed — screen may be frozen
fix_steps: Check Ethernet cable at HMI and switch — reseat both ends
Verify HMI has link LED lit — indicates physical connection
Ping PLC from HMI if possible (some HMIs have diagnostic page)
Check PLC status — resolve any PLC faults first
Power cycle HMI — turn off, wait 10 seconds, turn back on
If using serial (RS-232/485), check cable, termination, and baud rate
faults.field_trick: Power-cycle the HMI first. 50% of HMI comm faults are the HMI application hung, not a real network problem.

faults.name: Industrial Network Ring Break
faults.description: DLR (Device Level Ring) or MRP ring has detected a break. Network is running in line topology with no redundancy.
probable_causes: Cable disconnected or damaged in the ring
Network switch/device in the ring lost power or faulted
Port failure on one of the ring devices
New device added to ring without proper ring configuration
Cable length exceeded specification — signal degraded
fix_steps: Check ring supervisor status — most managed switches report ring status
Walk the ring path — identify which link is broken by checking link LEDs
If device lost power, restore power and verify ring closes
Check cable at identified break point — replace if damaged
Verify all devices in ring are configured for ring protocol (DLR/MRP)
After repair, verify ring status returns to Closed/Normal
faults.field_trick: The ring supervisor tells you which port is the active standby (the 'break' in a healthy ring). In a fault, compare which port went active — the break is between the last active device and the next one.

faults.name: UPS Battery Alarm
faults.description: UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) reports low battery, battery fault, or battery replace warning.
probable_causes: Batteries have reached end of life — typically 3-5 years
Battery charger malfunction — batteries not being maintained
Ambient temperature too high — accelerates battery degradation
One battery in string failed — dragging others down
UPS was running on battery during outage and batteries depleted
fix_steps: Check UPS display for specific battery alarm (low, replace, fault)
Run battery test from UPS panel or software — check runtime remaining
Inspect batteries — look for swelling, corrosion, or leaking
Measure individual battery voltages — identify weak cells
If batteries >3 years old and failing tests, schedule replacement
Verify charger is functioning — check charge voltage and current
Ensure UPS is in a climate-controlled environment
faults.field_trick: If batteries are over 3 years old and the replace light comes on, just replace them. Don't waste time testing — they're at the end of expected life.

faults.name: VFD Speed Deviation Fault
faults.description: Actual motor speed deviates from commanded speed by more than the allowable threshold for the configured timeout.
probable_causes: Mechanical overload causing motor to slow under load
Belt or coupling slipping between motor and driven equipment
Encoder feedback signal degraded — missing pulses
Drive current limit active — cannot deliver enough torque
Motor demagnetization in permanent magnet motors
fix_steps: Check mechanical load — reduce if possible and test
Inspect coupling and belts between motor and load
Verify encoder signal quality — check at drive diagnostic display
Check if drive is current-limiting — may need larger drive or motor
